base: UK EMPLOYEES IN RECEIPT of a bonus (Brightmine Compensation Planning payroll dataset), NOT % of employers operating a scheme. Receipt-rate is a reasonable lower-bound proxy for eligibility but is a DIFFERENT denominator - flagged. Year to May 2025.
notes: Brightmine Bonus Payments 2025: 12.2% of UK employees received a bonus in year to May 2025 - 3rd consecutive annual decline (19.7% 2023, 15.5% 2024, 12.2% 2025). By size: small firms 1-249 = 20.4% in receipt; large 1,000+ = 7.8% (small-firm staff ~2x as likely). DENOMINATOR CAUTION: this is employee RECEIPT rate, not employer SCHEME-PREVALENCE - eligibility (scheme exists) is typically higher than receipt (actually paid). question: Is there an approximate annual recognition budget per e
real_anchor: Annual gift-led recognition spend per employee (UK, directional): ~76% spend under GBP200/head/yr - 23.5% under GBP50, 52.9% GBP50-200, 9.4% GBP200-500, 3.5% GBP500-1,000; plus ~7% no programme / no formal budget. Per-head spend often LOWER at enterprise (budget spread thinner): ~30% of 1,000+ spend <GBP50/head.
source: Huggg "2026 UK Gifting & Recognition Benchmarks Report" (vendor survey via Typeform, fieldwork 26 Jan-10 Feb 2026, n=85 verified UK HR professionals at 80+ orgs). Grade C - SMALL SELF-SELECTED SAMPLE, vendor audience skewed to Tech/Software (31.8%) & Professional Services (23.5%); NOT nationally representative. Directional only.
base: UK HR professionals, n=85 (small sample), self-selected vendor audience, industry-skewed (tech/prof-services heavy). Employer-prevalence denominator (right type) but C-grade: treat as directional, not representative. Size splits (micro/small/mid/enterprise) in source.
notes: RESEARCH NOTE (NOT AN ANCHOR) - IES 'Non-Financial Recognition' (Silverman, 2004): design taxonomy only. Scheme types: praise / vouchers / nomination-based; tiered multi-site structures (region->annual->overall). Four common recognition criteria: excellent customer service, above-and-beyond, ideas/innovation, exceptional contribution/teamwork. Typical award value cited GBP 25-150 (2004 prices). Vouchers are taxable benefits - employer should cover tax/NIC. PREVALENCE figures in this paper are REJECTED as anchor-grade: secondary citations, 22-27yrs old, internally inconsistent (Rose 1998 75%, Brown&Armstrong 1999 ~50%, Bevan 2003 60%+). Grade D - age-failed, same basis as MetLife EBTS 2014. Row remains UNANCHORED pending a current primary UK prevalence source (REBA/XpertHR/Personnel Today/Workhuman UK). || SOURCE CHECKED & REJECTED for anchoring - Achievers State of Recognition 2026 (EMEA edition, UK/FR/DE EMPLOYEE-sentiment survey). All figures are employee FEELINGS (e.g. 17% feel recognised by manager, 92% would give extra effort, 15% recognised weekly) or recognised-employee reward-redemption choices (brand gift cards 37% etc.) - NOT employer PREVALENCE (% of orgs operating a scheme/budget). Wrong denominator + EMEA-blended. Recognition-cluster equivalent of the EAP error. Do not re-mine for prevalence. || NON-ANCHOR vendor-illustrative reference (NOT prevalence, NOT benchmarked): Reward Gateway blog (2018) suggests an ILLUSTRATIVE ~GBP75/employee/year recognition budget split as a pyramid - peer eCards unlimited/free, peer instant award GBP10-20, manager award GBP30-45, monthly/quarterly award GBP50-75, employee-of-year GBP90-125. This is a vendor-invented example, not a measured UK figure - do NOT anchor. Useful only as design reference for budget-tier structure. || SECONDARY-BENCHMARK CONTEXT (not a clean anchor - vendor-restated secondary, NOT primary prevalence): Incentivesmart cost guide (Apr 2026, R.White CEO) cites CIPD/SHRM benchmark of recognition spend 0.5-1% of payroll for a structured programme (0.25% basic / 0.5% structured / 0.75-1% high-performance), and typical UK R&R spend GBP50-150 per employee/year; platform tech GBP3-8 PEPM. Vendor restatement of secondary sources - use only as directional context, not a register anchor. || Huggg 2026 Grade C NEW ANCHOR (small sample n=85, vendor-skewed - directional). Budget distribution: 76.4% <GBP200/head. NOTE coexists with non-anchor refs already on row (Reward Gateway illustrative GBP75; Incentivesmart secondary 0.5-1% payroll / GBP50-150). Huggg is the only PRIMARY survey figure here but C-grade. || NON-ANCHOR (recommendation, not prevalence, USD/non-UK): O.C.Tanner vendor article restates PRESCRIPTIVE budget guidance - SHRM "at least 1% of payroll", Aon "0.25-3% of HR budget", RPI "$100-150/employee", O.C.Tanner "$200-350/employee". These are "should spend" recommendations in USD, NOT UK employer prevalence. Contrast with Huggg PRIMARY actuals (76% of UK orgs spend <GBP200/head) which IS the anchor. Do not anchor recommendations. || CORROBORATION (CIPD Reward Mgmt 2022, n=280): Christmas bonus/hamper/voucher/gifts offered by 58% (to-all 51%); other one-off bonus/hamper/voucher/gifts 52% (to-all 39%). SMEs MORE likely than large on Christmas gifts (58%) and non-Christmas one-offs (42%). This is reward-CURRENCY prevalence (gifting), distinct from a structured recognition BUDGET - reference for the recognition-currency picture, Grade B (2022 vintage). || CHECKED - NOT A PREVALENCE SOURCE (CIPD Incentives & Recognition: An Evidence Review, Jan 2022, ref 8205). This is an academic evidence synthesis on whether/how incentives+recognition work - NO UK employer-prevalence data of any kind. Does NOT fill the Brightmine recognition-budget gap. Useful only as design-principle reference (team-vs-individual allocation, 5-category rating fairness, distributive/procedural justice). Recognition-budget row stays Grade-C legacy.

notes: HMRC TAX REFERENCE (factual, not prevalence): long-service awards for 20+ years of service are exempt from tax up to GBP50 per year of service, provided non-cash and no similar award in previous 10 yrs (so a 25-year award is exempt up to GBP1,250).
